title: Elvis Presley Birthday
body: Elvis Presley was born on January 8, 1935, and his voice helped turn rock and roll into a global force. Even people who never owned one of his records still know the silhouette, the swagger, and the name.
notes: AI-drafted starter entry; verify exact source/date later.

title: Led Zeppelin I Arrives
body: Led Zeppelin’s debut album landed in January 1969 and immediately announced a louder, heavier version of rock. It is one of those first albums that somehow already sounds like a greatest-hits statement.
notes: AI-drafted starter entry; verify exact source/date later.

title: The Doors Debut
body: The Doors released their self-titled debut in early January 1967, giving rock one of its most distinctive first albums. Between Jim Morrison’s voice and Ray Manzarek’s keyboard sound, it never feels like anybody else.
notes: AI-drafted starter entry; verify exact source/date later.

title: Bob Marley Birthday
body: Bob Marley was born on February 6, 1945, and became one of the most recognizable musical voices in the world. His songs carried reggae, politics, and spiritual calm into mainstream popular culture.
notes: AI-drafted starter entry; verify exact source/date later.

title: Beatles in America
body: The Beatles arrived in the United States in February 1964, just before Beatlemania fully exploded on American television. It is one of the clearest dividing lines between pop before the British Invasion and pop after it.
notes: AI-drafted starter entry; verify exact source/date later.

title: Ed Sullivan Beatlemania
body: The Beatles’ first appearance on The Ed Sullivan Show in February 1964 became one of television’s most famous music moments. It helped make screaming fandom look like a national event instead of a passing fad.
notes: AI-drafted starter entry; verify exact source/date later.

title: Van Halen Debut
body: Van Halen’s debut album arrived in February 1978 and instantly made Eddie Van Halen’s guitar style impossible to ignore. It is the kind of album that sounds like a band kicking the front door open.
notes: AI-drafted starter entry; verify exact source/date later.

title: Fleetwood Mac Rumours
body: Rumours reached listeners in early February 1977 and became one of rock’s most famous breakup albums. All the tension in the band somehow turned into an unusually polished set of songs.
notes: AI-drafted starter entry; verify exact source/date later.

title: Dark Side of the Moon
body: Pink Floyd released The Dark Side of the Moon in March 1973, and it became one of the most durable albums in rock history. Its sound is still so smooth and atmospheric that it feels made for headphones half a century later.
notes: AI-drafted starter entry; verify exact source/date later.

title: Aretha Franklin Birthday
body: Aretha Franklin was born on March 25, 1942, and earned the nickname Queen of Soul for very good reason. Her voice could sound elegant, fierce, playful, and absolutely unbeatable all at once.
notes: AI-drafted starter entry; verify exact source/date later.

title: Eric Clapton Birthday
body: Eric Clapton was born on March 30, 1945, and became one of the best-known guitarists of the rock era. Few musicians have been attached to so many famous bands, solos, and reinventions.
notes: AI-drafted starter entry; verify exact source/date later.

title: Elvis Enters the Army
body: Elvis Presley entered the U.S. Army in March 1958, which was a huge cultural story at the time. It briefly turned the king of rock and roll into a symbol of normal American duty.
notes: AI-drafted starter entry; verify exact source/date later.

title: Beatles Breakup News
body: Paul McCartney publicly announced in April 1970 that he was no longer working with the Beatles, and the breakup suddenly felt real to fans. It remains one of the saddest and most famous endings in music history.
notes: AI-drafted starter entry; verify exact source/date later.

title: B.B. King Birthday
body: B.B. King was born on September 16, 1925, but his spring touring legacy still helped define the blues-rock bridge for countless guitarists. Even rock players who sounded nothing like him usually learned from him anyway.
notes: AI-drafted starter entry; verify exact source/date later.

title: Let It Be Album
body: The Beatles released Let It Be in May 1970, only weeks after the breakup felt official. That gives the album a bittersweet quality, like hearing the curtain fall while the songs are still playing.
notes: AI-drafted starter entry; verify exact source/date later.

title: Bob Dylan Birthday
body: Bob Dylan was born on May 24, 1941, and changed what people thought popular songwriting could do. He made lyrics feel capable of carrying poetry, sarcasm, protest, and mystery all at once.
notes: AI-drafted starter entry; verify exact source/date later.

title: Tommy Reaches the World
body: The Who’s Tommy landed in late spring 1969 and helped make the rock opera feel like a real commercial idea. It proved that a loud band could also think in narrative scale.
notes: AI-drafted starter entry; verify exact source/date later.

title: Sgt. Pepper Week
body: Sgt. Pepper’s Lonely Hearts Club Band arrived in early June 1967 and became one of the most discussed albums ever released. It is still shorthand for the moment when pop ambition started dressing like art.
notes: AI-drafted starter entry; verify exact source/date later.

title: Monterey Pop Festival
body: The Monterey Pop Festival opened in June 1967 and introduced many listeners to a new wave of psychedelic and guitar-driven stars. It also helped cement the festival as one of rock’s signature rituals.
notes: AI-drafted starter entry; verify exact source/date later.

title: David Bowie and Ziggy
body: The Rise and Fall of Ziggy Stardust and the Spiders from Mars arrived in June 1972 and gave David Bowie one of rock’s great alter egos. It feels theatrical, stylish, and still a little strange in the best way.
notes: AI-drafted starter entry; verify exact source/date later.

title: Jim Morrison Dies
body: Jim Morrison died in Paris in July 1971 at only 27 years old, adding to rock’s mythology of brilliant and short-lived frontmen. His legend remains tied to the Doors’ mix of danger, poetry, and swagger.
notes: AI-drafted starter entry; verify exact source/date later.

title: The First Elvis Single
body: That’s All Right began its journey in July 1954 and helped launch Elvis Presley’s recording career. Moments like that are reminders that rock history often starts in tiny studios before it reaches giant stages.
notes: AI-drafted starter entry; verify exact source/date later.

title: Mick Jagger Birthday
body: Mick Jagger was born on July 26, 1943, and somehow became one of rock’s permanent motion machines. Even people who roll their eyes at the swagger still recognize it instantly.
notes: AI-drafted starter entry; verify exact source/date later.

title: Jerry Garcia Birthday
body: Jerry Garcia was born on August 1, 1942, and became the most recognizable face of the Grateful Dead. His legacy is tied as much to community and live culture as to any single song.
notes: AI-drafted starter entry; verify exact source/date later.

title: Abbey Road Crosswalk
body: The famous Abbey Road cover photo was taken in August 1969 and turned an ordinary crosswalk into pilgrimage territory for music fans. Few album covers have done more with such a simple walk across the street.
notes: AI-drafted starter entry; verify exact source/date later.

title: Woodstock Opens
body: Woodstock opened in mid-August 1969 and became the most mythologized festival in rock history. The traffic, the mud, and the lineup all merged into one giant story about a generation trying to define itself.
notes: AI-drafted starter entry; verify exact source/date later.

title: Born to Run Breakthrough
body: Bruce Springsteen’s Born to Run arrived in late August 1975 and pushed him toward household-name status. The album sounds like somebody trying to fit the whole American night into one record.
notes: AI-drafted starter entry; verify exact source/date later.

title: Freddie Mercury Birthday
body: Freddie Mercury was born on September 5, 1946, and became one of rock’s most magnetic frontmen. He could be grand, funny, dramatic, and technically brilliant without ever seeming cautious.
notes: AI-drafted starter entry; verify exact source/date later.

title: Jimi Hendrix Remembered
body: Jimi Hendrix died in September 1970, but his influence on guitar never really went anywhere. He made virtuosity sound adventurous instead of merely impressive.
notes: AI-drafted starter entry; verify exact source/date later.

title: Bruce Springsteen Birthday
body: Bruce Springsteen was born on September 23, 1949, and later became one of rock’s most durable storytellers. His songs often feel like little American movies with guitars attached.
notes: AI-drafted starter entry; verify exact source/date later.

title: Janis Joplin Remembered
body: Janis Joplin died in early October 1970, but her voice still sounds like it is tearing right through the studio walls. She remains one of rock’s most distinctive and emotionally direct singers.
notes: AI-drafted starter entry; verify exact source/date later.

title: John Lennon Birthday
body: John Lennon was born on October 9, 1940, and became one of the central songwriting minds of the Beatles. His post-Beatles life only made his public image more complex and more lasting.
notes: AI-drafted starter entry; verify exact source/date later.

title: Bohemian Rhapsody Takes Flight
body: Queen released Bohemian Rhapsody at the end of October 1975, and a six-minute theatrical single somehow became a huge hit. It is still one of the best examples of a song succeeding by being gloriously over the top.
notes: AI-drafted starter entry; verify exact source/date later.

title: Neil Young Birthday
body: Neil Young was born on November 12, 1945, and built one of rock’s most unusual careers by refusing to sit still stylistically. Folk calm and distorted chaos both seem completely natural in his catalog.
notes: AI-drafted starter entry; verify exact source/date later.

title: The Last Waltz Week
body: The Band’s farewell concert known as The Last Waltz took place around Thanksgiving in 1976 and later became one of rock’s most beloved concert films. It turned a goodbye show into an all-star piece of musical memory.
notes: AI-drafted starter entry; verify exact source/date later.

title: Freddie Mercury Remembered
body: Freddie Mercury died in November 1991, only a day after publicly confirming he had AIDS. His death became one of the most widely felt losses in modern rock.
notes: AI-drafted starter entry; verify exact source/date later.

title: The Wall Arrives
body: Pink Floyd’s The Wall came out at the end of November 1979 and turned alienation into arena-sized spectacle. It is one of those albums that feels both intimate and gigantic at the same time.
notes: AI-drafted starter entry; verify exact source/date later.

title: John Lennon Remembered
body: John Lennon was killed in New York on December 8, 1980, ending one of the most remarkable public artistic lives of the rock era. His songs and image remain impossible to separate from the larger story of twentieth-century music.
notes: AI-drafted starter entry; verify exact source/date later.

title: Hotel California Season
body: Hotel California arrived in the winter of 1976 and gave the Eagles one of rock’s most recognizable album titles and title tracks. The phrase itself became shorthand for something glamorous, uneasy, and hard to leave behind.
notes: AI-drafted starter entry; verify exact source/date later.

title: Led Zeppelin Calls It a Day
body: Led Zeppelin announced in December 1980 that the band would not continue after John Bonham’s death. It felt like one of rock’s clearest cases of a group deciding that a key piece simply could not be replaced.
notes: AI-drafted starter entry; verify exact source/date later.

title: Ireland Joins the EEC
body: Ireland joined the European Economic Community on January 1, 1973, linking the state more deeply to Europe. It helped reshape trade, infrastructure, and the broader direction of the modern economy.
notes: AI-drafted starter entry; verify exact source/date later.

title: Women’s Christmas
body: January 6 is associated with Nollaig na mBan, or Women’s Christmas, a custom tied to the close of the holiday season. It remains one of the loveliest examples of older Irish domestic traditions surviving into modern life.
notes: AI-drafted starter entry; verify exact source/date later.

title: Douglas Hyde Birthday Week
body: Douglas Hyde was born in January 1860 and later became both a language revival leader and the first President of Ireland. He is a great example of culture and statehood crossing paths in one life.
notes: AI-drafted starter entry; verify exact source/date later.

title: First Dáil Week
body: Dáil Éireann first met in Dublin in January 1919, marking a major political moment in the struggle for independence. It remains one of the clearest institutional milestones in modern Irish history.
notes: AI-drafted starter entry; verify exact source/date later.

title: St. Brigid and Imbolc
body: February 1 is associated with both St. Brigid’s Day and the older festival of Imbolc. The overlap is a perfect reminder of how Christian and pre-Christian traditions often sit side by side in Irish memory.
notes: AI-drafted starter entry; verify exact source/date later.

title: James Joyce Birthday
body: James Joyce was born in Dublin on February 2, 1882, and later made the city one of literature’s most vividly mapped places. Few writers are so permanently tied to a single city’s identity.
notes: AI-drafted starter entry; verify exact source/date later.

title: Trinity College Charter Week
body: Trinity College Dublin received its charter in early March 1592 and grew into one of Ireland’s best-known institutions of learning. Its long history makes it both a university and a symbol in the national story.
notes: AI-drafted starter entry; verify exact source/date later.

title: Lady Gregory Birthday
body: Lady Gregory was born on March 15, 1852, and became one of the central figures of the Irish Literary Revival. Her influence behind the scenes was crucial to the Abbey Theatre and modern Irish drama.
notes: AI-drafted starter entry; verify exact source/date later.

title: St. Patrick’s Day
body: March 17 began as a feast day for St. Patrick and later became the world’s most recognizable celebration of Irish identity. It is one of those rare holidays that is both local in origin and global in reach.
notes: AI-drafted starter entry; verify exact source/date later.

title: Dana’s Eurovision Win Week
body: Dana’s Eurovision win in April 1970 started Ireland’s long and unusually successful relationship with the contest. It is a cheerful little reminder that national cultural mythology is not built only from politics and war.
notes: AI-drafted starter entry; verify exact source/date later.

title: Titanic at Cobh
body: The Titanic made its final port of call at Queenstown, now Cobh, in April 1912. That brief stop is why one of the world’s most famous maritime stories still has such a strong Irish link.
notes: AI-drafted starter entry; verify exact source/date later.

title: Republic of Ireland Act Week
body: The Republic of Ireland Act came into effect in April 1949 and clarified the state’s constitutional status. It marked another step away from older British structures and toward a more distinct modern identity.
notes: AI-drafted starter entry; verify exact source/date later.

title: Easter Rising Begins
body: The Easter Rising began in Dublin on April 24, 1916, and quickly became one of the most discussed events in Irish history. Its immediate military effect was limited, but its later political meaning was enormous.
notes: AI-drafted starter entry; verify exact source/date later.

title: Riverdance Debut
body: Riverdance made its first huge international impression during the Eurovision interval performance in April 1994. It turned Irish dance into a global stage phenomenon almost overnight.
notes: AI-drafted starter entry; verify exact source/date later.

title: Bealtaine Week
body: Early May is tied to Bealtaine, the old seasonal marker for the beginning of summer. It is one of the clearest reminders that older Irish life followed a rhythm shaped by light, weather, and the land.
notes: AI-drafted starter entry; verify exact source/date later.

title: Aer Lingus Takes Off
body: Aer Lingus operated its first flight in May 1936 and became one of the most recognizable symbols of modern Irish travel. A national airline can tell you a lot about how a country imagines itself in motion.
notes: AI-drafted starter entry; verify exact source/date later.

title: Thomas Moore Birthday
body: Thomas Moore was born on May 28, 1779, and his songs and poems became staples of nineteenth-century Irish cultural life. He is a good reminder that popularity and literary influence can travel together.
notes: AI-drafted starter entry; verify exact source/date later.

title: W.B. Yeats Birthday
body: W.B. Yeats was born on June 13, 1865, and later became one of Ireland’s most celebrated writers. He helped turn Irish myth, politics, and modern poetry into a single dazzling career.
notes: AI-drafted starter entry; verify exact source/date later.

title: Bloomsday
body: June 16 is celebrated as Bloomsday in honor of James Joyce’s Ulysses. It may be the best example anywhere of a city lovingly turning a novel into an annual public holiday mood.
notes: AI-drafted starter entry; verify exact source/date later.

title: Douglas Hyde Inaugurated
body: Douglas Hyde became the first President of Ireland in June 1938, linking scholarship, language revival, and national symbolism in one office. It was a fitting choice for a new state still defining itself.
notes: AI-drafted starter entry; verify exact source/date later.

title: Constitution Plebiscite Week
body: Bunreacht na hÉireann was approved by plebiscite in July 1937 and became the basis of the modern Irish state. Constitutions are not usually glamorous, but they quietly shape everyday life for generations.
notes: AI-drafted starter entry; verify exact source/date later.

title: George Bernard Shaw Birthday
body: George Bernard Shaw was born on July 26, 1856, in Dublin and went on to become one of the most famous playwrights in the English language. His wit still feels sharp enough to nick a finger.
notes: AI-drafted starter entry; verify exact source/date later.

title: Harry Clarke Birthday Week
body: Harry Clarke was born at the end of July 1889 and became one of Ireland’s most distinctive visual artists. His stained glass and illustrations have a dreamy, jewel-like intensity that is hard to mistake.
notes: AI-drafted starter entry; verify exact source/date later.

title: Lughnasadh
body: August 1 is traditionally linked with Lughnasadh, one of the old Gaelic seasonal festivals. Harvest time and community gathering were once so deeply connected that the calendar itself felt social.
notes: AI-drafted starter entry; verify exact source/date later.

title: Puck Fair Week
body: Puck Fair arrives around August in Killorglin and remains one of Ireland’s most unusual festival traditions. Any country willing to crown a goat as part of the festivities deserves some affection.
notes: AI-drafted starter entry; verify exact source/date later.

title: The Emergency Begins
body: The period known as The Emergency began in September 1939 as Ireland navigated the Second World War with official neutrality. Even the name itself shows how carefully the state framed that tense era.
notes: AI-drafted starter entry; verify exact source/date later.

title: Michael Collins Week
body: Michael Collins is closely remembered each August and September because of the Civil War era and his death in 1922. He remains one of the most debated and mythologized figures in the Irish revolutionary story.
notes: AI-drafted starter entry; verify exact source/date later.

title: Oscar Wilde Birthday
body: Oscar Wilde was born in Dublin on October 16, 1854, and became one of the most quoted writers in English. His best lines are so polished that people still borrow them a century later.
notes: AI-drafted starter entry; verify exact source/date later.

title: Samhain Week
body: Late October is tied to Samhain, the seasonal turning point most often linked to the older roots of Halloween. It is a wonderfully Irish example of how a serious seasonal threshold can later wear a costume.
notes: AI-drafted starter entry; verify exact source/date later.

title: GAA Founded
body: The Gaelic Athletic Association was founded on November 1, 1884, in Thurles and became one of the most important institutions in Irish sporting life. In Ireland, sport is often also geography, memory, and local pride.
notes: AI-drafted starter entry; verify exact source/date later.

title: Bram Stoker Birthday
body: Bram Stoker was born in Dublin on November 8, 1847, and later wrote Dracula. It is one of those delightful Irish cultural facts that one of literature’s great vampires came from Dublin.
notes: AI-drafted starter entry; verify exact source/date later.

title: C.S. Lewis Birthday
body: C.S. Lewis was born in Belfast on November 29, 1898, and became one of the twentieth century’s most widely read authors. His work shows how Irish-born writers often travelled far while keeping strong roots in place.
notes: AI-drafted starter entry; verify exact source/date later.

title: Jonathan Swift Birthday Week
body: Jonathan Swift was born at the end of November 1667 and remains one of the sharpest satirists associated with Ireland. Satire is usually funniest when it is also a little dangerous, and Swift understood that perfectly.
notes: AI-drafted starter entry; verify exact source/date later.

title: Irish Free State Begins
body: The Irish Free State officially came into existence on December 6, 1922. It was not the final answer to every constitutional question, but it was a major turning point in modern Irish history.
notes: AI-drafted starter entry; verify exact source/date later.

title: Irish Nobel Week
body: December is a nice week to remember that Irish writers such as W.B. Yeats, Samuel Beckett, and Seamus Heaney all became Nobel laureates. For a small island, Ireland has an astonishing literary batting average.
notes: AI-drafted starter entry; verify exact source/date later.

title: Newgrange Solstice
body: The winter solstice is most famously linked in Ireland with the illuminated passage at Newgrange. It remains one of the great reminders that prehistoric builders could think with beauty, astronomy, and stone all at once.
notes: AI-drafted starter entry; verify exact source/date later.

title: Abbey Theatre Opens
body: The Abbey Theatre opened in Dublin in late December 1904 and became central to modern Irish drama. Few national theatres are so deeply entangled with the idea of cultural self-definition.
notes: AI-drafted starter entry; verify exact source/date later.

title: Constitution Takes Effect
body: Bunreacht na hÉireann came into effect on December 29, 1937, and remains the foundation of Irish constitutional life. Even people who never read a constitution still live inside its assumptions.
notes: AI-drafted starter entry; verify exact source/date later.

title: Arthur Guinness Lease
body: Arthur Guinness signed the famous St. James’s Gate lease on December 31, 1759, creating one of Ireland’s most quoted origin stories. A lease is not usually romantic, but this one somehow became legend.
notes: AI-drafted starter entry; verify exact source/date later.

title: Saint Columba Week
body: Early summer is a good time to remember St. Columba, the Irish missionary monk born in the sixth century who helped shape Christian life in Scotland. His story is part of the older web connecting Ireland to the wider Gaelic world.
notes: AI-drafted starter entry; verify exact source/date later.

title: Book of Kells Season
body: Spring is a lovely season to remember the Book of Kells, the illuminated manuscript created around the year 800. It remains one of the most famous examples of Irish artistry surviving from the early medieval world.
notes: AI-drafted starter entry; verify exact source/date later.

title: Skellig Michael Week
body: Late summer travel talk often brings up Skellig Michael, the dramatic monastic island off Kerry. It is one of those places that feels almost invented by a novelist and yet is very real.
notes: AI-drafted starter entry; verify exact source/date later.

title: Phoenix Park Week
body: Summer is a nice moment to remember that Dublin’s Phoenix Park is one of Europe’s great urban parks. It manages to be official, historic, and pleasantly rambling all at the same time.
notes: AI-drafted starter entry; verify exact source/date later.

title: Fenway Park Opens
body: Fenway Park opened in April 1912 and remains one of baseball’s most recognizable homes. The Green Monster alone would have been enough to make it memorable, but the whole place feels like baseball wearing old brick and stubbornness.
notes: AI-drafted starter entry; verify exact source/date later.

title: Bill Russell Birthday
body: Bill Russell was born on February 12, 1934, and later became the defining champion of Celtics history. Eleven titles as a player is the kind of number that still looks slightly unreal.
notes: AI-drafted starter entry; verify exact source/date later.

title: Patriots First Dynasty Title
body: The Patriots won Super Bowl XXXVI on February 3, 2002, beginning the franchise’s long championship era. It also launched one of the most talked-about coach-quarterback partnerships in sports history.
notes: AI-drafted starter entry; verify exact source/date later.

title: Patriots Win Another in Arizona
body: The Patriots beat the Seahawks in Super Bowl XLIX in February 2015, sealing the game with Malcolm Butler’s famous interception. It was one of those endings that turned instant shock into instant legend.
notes: AI-drafted starter entry; verify exact source/date later.

title: The 28–3 Comeback
body: The Patriots’ comeback win over Atlanta in Super Bowl LI in February 2017 remains one of the wildest title games ever played. Being down 28–3 and still winning feels almost fictional when you say it out loud.
notes: AI-drafted starter entry; verify exact source/date later.

title: Bobby Orr Birthday
body: Bobby Orr was born on March 20, 1948, and changed what people expected from a defenseman. Bruins history is full of great names, but his still lands with a little extra electricity.
notes: AI-drafted starter entry; verify exact source/date later.

title: John Havlicek Birthday
body: John Havlicek was born on April 8, 1940, and became one of the most beloved all-around Celtics. His endurance and reliability made him feel like the kind of player every coach dreams about.
notes: AI-drafted starter entry; verify exact source/date later.

title: Patriots’ Day and Boston
body: Patriots’ Day is inseparable from Boston sports culture because it brings both the Boston Marathon and a traditional Red Sox day game. It is one of those local civic rituals that makes the sports calendar feel woven into the city itself.
notes: AI-drafted starter entry; verify exact source/date later.

title: Babe Ruth Heads to New York
body: The Red Sox sold Babe Ruth to the Yankees in the winter of 1919, a move that later gained almost mythic status in Boston baseball lore. Few transactions have been retold with more drama, superstition, and regret.
notes: AI-drafted starter entry; verify exact source/date later.

title: Bill Belichick Birthday
body: Bill Belichick was born on April 16, 1952, and later became the most successful coach in Patriots history. His public style was famously dry, but his teams usually did the louder talking.
notes: AI-drafted starter entry; verify exact source/date later.

title: Boston Garden Legacy Week
body: The old Boston Garden opened in November 1928 and became one of the most storied venues in American sports. Even people who never set foot inside it know the building by mood alone.
notes: AI-drafted starter entry; verify exact source/date later.

title: Celtics Are Born
body: The Celtics were founded in June 1946 and went on to become one of basketball’s signature franchises. Their history is so crowded with banners that the rafters almost feel like a second record book.
notes: AI-drafted starter entry; verify exact source/date later.

title: Bruins Are Born
body: The Bruins were founded in November 1924 and became the first American team in the NHL. That fact alone gives them a permanent place in hockey history before you even get to the trophies.
notes: AI-drafted starter entry; verify exact source/date later.

title: Patriots Franchise Begins
body: Boston was awarded an AFL franchise in late 1959, laying the groundwork for what became the Patriots. Dynasties always look inevitable in hindsight, but they begin as simple franchise paperwork.
notes: AI-drafted starter entry; verify exact source/date later.

title: Celtics Banner in 2008
body: The Celtics won the 2008 NBA title on June 17, giving the Big Three era its crowning achievement. It felt like old Boston basketball pride getting a fresh coat of green paint.
notes: AI-drafted starter entry; verify exact source/date later.

title: Bruins Cup in 2011
body: The Bruins won the Stanley Cup on June 15, 2011, ending a long championship drought. A parade through Boston is always loud, but a Cup parade carries a special kind of growl.
notes: AI-drafted starter entry; verify exact source/date later.

title: David Ortiz Birthday
body: David Ortiz was born on November 18, 1975, and became one of the great October heroes in Red Sox history. Big Papi is one of those nicknames that somehow fit both the player and the city perfectly.
notes: AI-drafted starter entry; verify exact source/date later.

title: Tom Brady Birthday
body: Tom Brady was born on August 3, 1977, and later became the face of the Patriots dynasty. It is still funny to remember that one of the biggest stars in NFL history began as a sixth-round pick.
notes: AI-drafted starter entry; verify exact source/date later.

title: Carl Yastrzemski Birthday
body: Carl Yastrzemski was born on August 22, 1939, and became one of the great long-career symbols of Red Sox baseball. His name feels stitched right into the franchise’s old fabric.
notes: AI-drafted starter entry; verify exact source/date later.

title: Ted Williams Birthday
body: Ted Williams was born on August 30, 1918, and remains the last player to hit .400 in a Major League season. In Boston, numbers like that do not feel statistical so much as mythical.
notes: AI-drafted starter entry; verify exact source/date later.

title: Red Auerbach Birthday
body: Red Auerbach was born on September 20, 1917, and became one of the most influential figures in Celtics history. The victory cigar alone made him look like a coach who expected the ending to go his way.
notes: AI-drafted starter entry; verify exact source/date later.

title: Carlton Fisk’s Wave-It-Fair Moment
body: Carlton Fisk’s dramatic homer in Game 6 of the 1975 World Series remains one of the most replayed swings in Red Sox history. The body language was almost as famous as the ball flight.
notes: AI-drafted starter entry; verify exact source/date later.

title: The Curse Breaks
body: The Red Sox won the 2004 World Series on October 27, ending the franchise’s most famous championship drought. Even people who hate the phrase 'curse' understand why Boston still savors that night.
notes: AI-drafted starter entry; verify exact source/date later.

title: Another Red Sox Title
body: The Red Sox swept the Rockies to win the 2007 World Series in late October. After 2004, the surprise was not that Boston could win again but how quickly winning started to feel familiar.
notes: AI-drafted starter entry; verify exact source/date later.

title: Bearded Boston Champions
body: The Red Sox won the 2013 World Series on October 30, finishing one of the most emotionally resonant seasons in franchise history. The team’s beard-heavy look only made the whole run more cartoonishly memorable.
notes: AI-drafted starter entry; verify exact source/date later.

title: Another Parade in 2018
body: The Red Sox won the 2018 World Series at the end of October, adding another title to a once-starving fan base that now had to keep track of multiple recent parades. History changes the emotional temperature of a city.
notes: AI-drafted starter entry; verify exact source/date later.

title: Larry Bird Birthday
body: Larry Bird was born on December 7, 1956, and became one of the defining stars of Celtics basketball. His game looked unhurried, but his competitive edge was anything but relaxed.
notes: AI-drafted starter entry; verify exact source/date later.

title: Pedro Martínez Birthday
body: Pedro Martínez was born on October 25, 1971, and brought one of the most electric peaks any Red Sox pitcher has ever had. At his best, hitters looked like they were guessing and apologizing at the same time.
notes: AI-drafted starter entry; verify exact source/date later.

title: Patrice Bergeron Birthday
body: Patrice Bergeron was born on July 24, 1985, and became the kind of Bruins captain every fan base thinks it deserves. His game made intelligence, responsibility, and timing look quietly glamorous.

title: Paul Pierce Birthday
body: Paul Pierce was born on October 13, 1977, and later gave the Celtics one of their great modern stars. He had a gift for looking slightly irritated and completely ready at the same time.

title: Julian Edelman Birthday

title: Rob Gronkowski Birthday
body: Rob Gronkowski was born on May 14, 1989, and brought a kind of joyful chaos to Patriots football. He made being both dominant and silly look like a perfectly normal combination.

title: Cam Neely Birthday
body: Cam Neely was born on June 6, 1965, and became one of the most beloved power forwards in Bruins history. Bruins fans tend to appreciate players who look like they enjoy a little healthy menace.

title: Ray Bourque Birthday
body: Ray Bourque was born on December 28, 1960, and remains one of the greatest defensemen in NHL history. His long Bruins career made him feel less like a player and more like a permanent part of the blue line itself.

title: Jayson Tatum Birthday
body: Jayson Tatum was born on March 3, 1998, and became one of the modern cornerstones of Celtics basketball. Every era of Boston sports likes to imagine its next long-running star, and he fits the part nicely.

title: Dustin Pedroia Birthday
body: Dustin Pedroia was born on August 17, 1983, and became one of the most stubbornly energetic players of his Red Sox era. He played like every inning had personally offended him.

title: Nomar Garciaparra Birthday
body: Nomar Garciaparra was born on July 23, 1973, and for a while felt like the beating heart of Red Sox baseball in the late 1990s. His batting-glove routine alone could identify him from a mile away.

title: Martin Luther King Jr. Birthday
body: Martin Luther King Jr. was born on January 15, 1929, and became the most recognized voice of the American civil rights movement. His speeches remain powerful because they combine moral force with extraordinary clarity.

title: Benjamin Franklin Birthday
body: Benjamin Franklin was born on January 17, 1706, and somehow managed to be a printer, inventor, writer, diplomat, and professional collector of good one-liners. He is the kind of historical figure who seems to have lived two or three full lives at once.
notes: AI-drafted starter entry; verify exact source/date later.

title: Wolfgang Amadeus Mozart Birthday
body: Mozart was born on January 27, 1756, and became one of the most famous composers in Western music. The astonishing part is not only how good the music is but how much of it he created in such a short life.
notes: AI-drafted starter entry; verify exact source/date later.

title: Charles Dickens Birthday
body: Charles Dickens was born on February 7, 1812, and later gave the English-speaking world some of its most memorable fictional characters. Even people who have never finished one of his novels usually know a Dickens character by name.
notes: AI-drafted starter entry; verify exact source/date later.

title: Abraham Lincoln Birthday
body: Abraham Lincoln was born on February 12, 1809, and remains one of the central figures in American history. His public writing was so clear and spare that it still feels modern.
notes: AI-drafted starter entry; verify exact source/date later.

title: George Washington Birthday
body: George Washington was born on February 22, 1732, and became the first president of the United States. Founding figures can seem distant, but Washington still feels oddly vivid as a symbol of restraint and precedent.

title: Frederic Chopin Birthday Week
body: Chopin was born around the start of March 1810 and became one of the great poet-composers of the piano. His music often sounds delicate until you notice how emotionally tough it really is.

title: Albert Einstein Birthday
body: Albert Einstein was born on March 14, 1879, and later transformed modern physics. It is slightly funny that one of the world’s most recognizable geniuses is also one of its most recognizable haircuts.

title: Vincent van Gogh Birthday
body: Vincent van Gogh was born on March 30, 1853, and became one of the world’s most beloved painters only after his death. His work is now so familiar that it is easy to forget how unconventional it once seemed.

title: Maya Angelou Birthday
body: Maya Angelou was born on April 4, 1928, and became one of the most admired American writers and public voices of the twentieth century. She had a rare gift for sounding both elegant and direct at the same time.

title: Booker T. Washington Birthday
body: Booker T. Washington was born on April 5, 1856, and became one of the most influential educators in American history. His life is often used to talk about education not just as schooling but as strategy.

title: Bette Davis Birthday
body: Bette Davis was born on April 5, 1908, and became one of classic Hollywood’s fiercest screen presences. She could make a glance feel like a line of dialogue.
notes: AI-drafted starter entry; verify exact source/date later.

title: William Shakespeare Birthday Week
body: Shakespeare is traditionally celebrated around April 23, and his work still dominates stages and classrooms around the world. Very few writers have contributed so many phrases that people use without realizing where they came from.
notes: AI-drafted starter entry; verify exact source/date later.

title: Ella Fitzgerald Birthday
body: Ella Fitzgerald was born on April 25, 1917, and became one of the great voices in jazz. Her phrasing could sound effortless even when the music underneath it was anything but simple.

title: Audrey Hepburn Birthday
body: Audrey Hepburn was born on May 4, 1929, and became a film icon with unusual grace and lightness. Her later humanitarian work kept her public image from feeling merely glamorous.

title: Florence Nightingale Birthday
body: Florence Nightingale was born on May 12, 1820, and helped transform nursing into a modern profession. It is rare for one person’s reputation to be tied so strongly to both compassion and data.
notes: AI-drafted starter entry; verify exact source/date later.

title: Malcolm X Birthday
body: Malcolm X was born on May 19, 1925, and became one of the most influential and debated American speakers of the twentieth century. His life still prompts people to think about change, conviction, and public honesty.

title: Bob Dylan Birthday
body: Bob Dylan was born on May 24, 1941, and changed what people thought lyrics in popular music could do. He made listeners pay closer attention to words without sacrificing mystery.

title: John F. Kennedy Birthday
body: John F. Kennedy was born on May 29, 1917, and remains one of the most discussed presidents in modern American history. Public memory tends to keep him wrapped in both glamour and unfinished possibility.

title: Marilyn Monroe Birthday
body: Marilyn Monroe was born on June 1, 1926, and became one of the defining faces of twentieth-century celebrity. Her image is so famous that people sometimes forget she also had strong comic timing.

title: Judy Garland Birthday
body: Judy Garland was born on June 10, 1922, and became one of Hollywood’s most beloved performers. Her voice and vulnerability gave her a kind of emotional immediacy that still travels.

title: Anne Frank Birthday
body: Anne Frank was born on June 12, 1929, and her diary became one of the most widely read books of the twentieth century. Her voice remains powerful because it feels both ordinary and extraordinary at once.

title: Helen Keller Birthday
body: Helen Keller was born on June 27, 1880, and became a symbol of learning, communication, and determined public life. Her story still expands people’s ideas about what education can do.

title: Frida Kahlo Birthday
body: Frida Kahlo was born on July 6, 1907, and became one of the most recognizable painters of the twentieth century. Her self-portraits feel intimate, symbolic, and entirely unwilling to be ignored.

title: Nikola Tesla Birthday
body: Nikola Tesla was born on July 10, 1856, and became one of the most mythologized inventors in modern science history. He is a good reminder that genius and public image do not always behave neatly together.

title: Nelson Mandela Birthday
body: Nelson Mandela was born on July 18, 1918, and became one of the most admired political figures of the modern era. His public legacy is closely tied to endurance without bitterness.

title: Beatrix Potter Birthday
body: Beatrix Potter was born on July 28, 1866, and gave the world some of its most beloved animal characters. Her stories are gentle, but they are never quite as fluffy as people assume.

title: Lucille Ball Birthday
body: Lucille Ball was born on August 6, 1911, and became one of television’s most influential comic performers. Physical comedy always looks easy until you watch someone truly great do it.

title: Alfred Hitchcock Birthday
body: Alfred Hitchcock was born on August 13, 1899, and became the most recognizable name in suspense cinema. He understood that fear is often more effective when it arrives one beat before the audience is ready.

title: Mother Teresa Birthday
body: Mother Teresa was born on August 26, 1910, and became one of the best-known humanitarian figures of the twentieth century. Her public image remains closely tied to service, discipline, and devotion.
notes: AI-drafted starter entry; verify exact source/date later.

title: Roald Dahl Birthday
body: Roald Dahl was born on September 13, 1916, and wrote children’s books that were often funny, dark, and gleefully strange. He understood that kids can handle much more mischief than adults sometimes think.

title: Agatha Christie Birthday
body: Agatha Christie was born on September 15, 1890, and became one of the most successful mystery writers ever. Her plots often feel like elegant little machines built to surprise politely.

title: Stephen King Birthday
body: Stephen King was born on September 21, 1947, and became one of the most widely read horror writers in modern fiction. One reason he works so well is that he usually starts somewhere ordinary before making it wonderfully weird.

title: Mahatma Gandhi Birthday
body: Mahatma Gandhi was born on October 2, 1869, and became one of the most influential political thinkers of the twentieth century. His name is still almost shorthand for nonviolent resistance.

title: Eleanor Roosevelt Birthday
body: Eleanor Roosevelt was born on October 11, 1884, and became one of the most influential first ladies in American history. She kept expanding the job until it looked less ceremonial and more public-minded.

title: Oscar Wilde Birthday
body: Oscar Wilde was born on October 16, 1854, and became one of the great masters of the polished insult and perfect epigram. Few writers are quoted so often by people who may not realize they are quoting him.

title: Pablo Picasso Birthday
body: Pablo Picasso was born on October 25, 1881, and changed modern art more than once. Very few artists have had both that much range and that much swagger.

title: Marie Curie Birthday
body: Marie Curie was born on November 7, 1867, and remains one of the most celebrated scientists in history. Her career still stands out because brilliance, persistence, and danger were all tangled together in the same work.

title: Astrid Lindgren Birthday
body: Astrid Lindgren was born on November 14, 1907, and created Pippi Longstocking, one of children’s literature’s great free spirits. Characters like that tend to outlive trends because independence never really goes out of style.

title: Mark Twain Birthday
body: Mark Twain was born on November 30, 1835, and became one of America’s sharpest comic writers. His humor usually works because it is laughing and glaring at the same time.

title: Walt Disney Birthday
body: Walt Disney was born on December 5, 1901, and built one of the world’s most influential entertainment empires. His cultural footprint is so large that it can be hard to separate the man from the kingdom.

title: Jane Austen Birthday
body: Jane Austen was born on December 16, 1775, and still feels surprisingly modern in her humor and observations. Her novels understand social awkwardness so well that they never really age out.

title: Isaac Newton Birthday Week
body: Isaac Newton is traditionally associated with a late-December or early-January birthday depending on calendar style. Either way, he remains one of the most important figures in the history of science.

title: Louis Pasteur Birthday
body: Louis Pasteur was born on December 27, 1822, and helped transform modern microbiology and medicine. Few scientists improved daily life so directly while also becoming a household name.

title: New Year Resolutions
body: The new year has long been treated as a good time to promise yourself better habits, a tradition that goes back much farther than modern planners and gym ads. Humans are apparently very attached to the idea that January deserves a fresh notebook.
notes: AI-drafted starter entry; verify exact source/date later.

title: Polar Bear Week
body: Early January always makes it a good moment to remember that polar bears are excellent swimmers despite looking built mainly for snow. They can cover surprising distances in cold water when searching for sea ice and food.
notes: AI-drafted starter entry; verify exact source/date later.

title: Groundhog Day
body: February 2 became Groundhog Day in the United States as a folkloric weather tradition with roots linked to older European customs. It is one of those holidays that survives largely because people enjoy pretending a furry meteorologist is plausible.
notes: AI-drafted starter entry; verify exact source/date later.

title: National Pizza Day
body: February 9 is often celebrated as National Pizza Day, which is convenient because pizza never really needed much help attracting admirers. Very few foods can start an argument about style and still make everybody hungry.

title: Leap Day Charm
body: Leap day only appears in leap years because the calendar needs occasional adjustment to stay lined up with Earth’s orbit. It is a wonderfully nerdy solution disguised as an excuse for birthday jokes.
notes: AI-drafted starter entry; verify exact source/date later.

title: Pi Day
body: March 14 is celebrated as Pi Day because 3/14 matches the beginning of the number 3.14. It is one of the few math holidays that comes with both irrational numbers and pie puns.
notes: AI-drafted starter entry; verify exact source/date later.

title: The Equinox
body: Around late March, day and night move closer to balance during the spring equinox. Seasonal turning points tend to feel poetic partly because astronomy is doing the staging for us.

title: April Fool’s Day
body: April 1 has a long reputation as a day for harmless pranks and practical jokes. The trick, of course, is that 'harmless' does a lot of the moral work in that sentence.
notes: AI-drafted starter entry; verify exact source/date later.

title: Bell Bottom Day
body: Early April brings National Bell Bottoms Day, a delightful reminder that fashion can leave and then march right back in again. Few garments announce their own enthusiasm as clearly as bell bottoms.

title: Deep Dish Pizza Day
body: April 5 is also associated with National Deep Dish Pizza Day, which means somebody somewhere is probably making a casserole-versus-pizza argument in earnest. Food observances are at their best when they sound like an excuse and a debate at the same time.

title: Earth Day
body: Earth Day is observed on April 22 and became one of the most recognizable environmental observances in the world. It works best when it inspires something practical instead of just a nicely worded caption.
notes: AI-drafted starter entry; verify exact source/date later.

title: May Day
body: May 1 has long been connected with spring festivals, flowers, and community celebrations in many places. Seasonal holidays often survive because people do not actually need much encouragement to celebrate warmth returning.

title: Cinco de Mayo Week
body: Early May often brings cheerful confusion in the United States about Cinco de Mayo, which marks a Mexican military victory at Puebla rather than independence day. It is a useful reminder that festive popularity and historical accuracy are not always best friends.

title: National Barbecue Day
body: May has plenty of outdoor-food energy, and National Barbecue Day fits that mood perfectly. Smoke, sauce, and arguments about regional style all arrive right on schedule.

title: The Summer Solstice
body: Late June brings the summer solstice, the longest day of the year in the Northern Hemisphere. It is one of those astronomical events that makes people suddenly aware they live on a spinning tilted planet.
notes: AI-drafted starter entry; verify exact source/date later.

title: Ice Cream Month Mood
body: July is National Ice Cream Month in the United States, which may be one of the least controversial official observances on record. Some celebrations are powerful because they set the bar exactly where people already wanted it.
notes: AI-drafted starter entry; verify exact source/date later.

title: Shark Week Energy
body: Sharks get a burst of attention every summer thanks to Shark Week and the general season of beach curiosity. They are a good example of animals that are both genuinely impressive and dramatically overblamed.

title: Moon Landing Week
body: July is always a nice time to remember that humans first walked on the Moon in 1969. Space history never stops sounding slightly impossible when you say it plainly.

title: National Watermelon Day
body: August 3 is associated with National Watermelon Day, which feels exactly appropriate for a month that already tastes like summer. Very few foods are this messy and this refreshing at the same time.

title: Left-Handers Day
body: August 13 is recognized as International Left-Handers Day, a tiny observance with excellent underdog energy. It is always nice when the calendar pauses to salute a minority trait instead of a major historical event.

title: The Perseids
body: Mid-August is prime time for the Perseid meteor shower, one of the most popular skywatching events of the year. Falling stars feel more magical when you remember they are also just bits of cosmic debris burning up overhead.

title: Talk Like a Pirate Day
body: September 19 is International Talk Like a Pirate Day, which remains one of the internet era’s most durable silly observances. Any holiday that improves with a bad accent deserves a little credit.

title: The Autumn Equinox
body: Late September brings the autumn equinox, when daylight and darkness pull closer to balance again. Even people who do not love summer’s end can usually appreciate the drama of the seasonal handoff.

title: National Coffee Day
body: September 29 is National Coffee Day, a holiday suspiciously likely to be supported by exhausted adults. If any beverage was going to earn a day of gratitude, coffee had a strong résumé.
notes: AI-drafted starter entry; verify exact source/date later.

title: Pumpkin Season
body: Early October marks the yearly moment when pumpkin flavor moves from crop to personality. Seasonal foods become especially funny when they also become branding strategies.

title: World Animal Day
body: October 4 is World Animal Day, a nice excuse to appreciate creatures that are beautiful, useful, odd, or all three at once. Humans are at their most charming when they make room for affection beyond their own species.

title: Boss’s Day
body: October 16 is associated with Boss’s Day, which usually lands somewhere between sincere appreciation and office comedy. Workplace holidays have a habit of revealing exactly how awkward adults can be while trying to be nice.

title: Halloween Roots
body: Halloween on October 31 mixes costumes, candy, and older seasonal traditions in a way that feels half spooky and half neighborhood block party. It may be the only holiday where sugar and theatrical darkness cooperate so well.
notes: AI-drafted starter entry; verify exact source/date later.

title: Daylight Saving Time Ends
body: Early November often brings the end of daylight saving time in much of the United States, which means the clocks change and everybody becomes a tiny bit dramatic about it. Few calendar adjustments inspire so much grumbling for something that takes one minute.
notes: AI-drafted starter entry; verify exact source/date later.

title: Sandwich Day
body: November 3 is National Sandwich Day, a holiday with almost unlimited practical application. Bread plus filling is not a complicated concept, but humanity has clearly decided it was a good one.

title: Origami Day
body: November 11 is World Origami Day, a charming little observance for the art of turning flat paper into something with surprise and structure. Folding a sheet into a crane is basically engineering with better manners.

title: Pie Season
body: Late November is peak pie season in many American kitchens, especially around Thanksgiving. Desserts linked to holidays always feel more serious because nostalgia is quietly doing half the baking.

title: Winter Solstice
body: The winter solstice in late December marks the shortest day of the year in the Northern Hemisphere. The nice part is that once you hit it, the light slowly starts sneaking back.
notes: AI-drafted starter entry; verify exact source/date later.

title: Ugly Sweater Energy
body: December has become prime ugly-sweater season, which means festive clothing starts competing to be intentionally tasteless. There is something strangely healthy about making bad style into a group activity.

title: New Year’s Eve Sparkle
body: New Year’s Eve turns the end of the calendar into a worldwide excuse for countdowns, glitter, and mild existential reflection. Few nights ask people to feel sentimental and loud at the same time quite so efficiently.

title: National Donut Day Mood
body: Early June often brings National Donut Day, which has a strong claim on public affection for obvious reasons. Round foods somehow seem even friendlier when they come with frosting.

title: Bird Migration Season
body: Spring and fall are both marvelous times to remember that bird migration is one of nature’s great recurring dramas. Tiny bodies crossing huge distances will never stop sounding impressive.
